question_paper_html:
Annual Exam - 2024

Code: 56/4/3 (set-3)

  1. Please check that this question paper contains 23 printed pages.
  2. Please check that this question paper contains 33 questions.
  3. Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  4. Please write down the serial number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it.
  5. 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the students will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:70

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions carefully and follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 33 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper is divided into five sections - Section A, B, C, D and E.
  3. Section A - questions number 1 to 16 are multiple choice type questions. Each question carries 1 mark.
  4. Section B - questions number 17 to 21 are very short answer type questions. Each question carries 2 marks.
  5. Section C - questions number 22 to 28 are short answer type questions. Each question carries 3 marks.
  6. Section D - questions number 29 and 30 are case-based questions. Each question carries 4 marks.
  7. Section E - questions number 31 to 33 are long answer type questions. Each question carries 5 marks.
  8. There is no overall choice given in the question paper. However, an internal choice has been provided in few questions in all the sections except Section A.
  9. Kindly note that there is a separate question paper for Visually Impaired candidates.
  10. Use of calculators is not allowed.

SECTION A

Question No. 1 to 16 are Multiple Choice type questions carrying 1 mark each. {tex}16 \times 1=16{/tex}
1.

The molar ionic conductivities of {tex}{Al}^{3+}{/tex} and {tex}{SO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex} are {tex}189 {~S} \ {cm}^{2} \ {mol}^{-1}{/tex} and {tex}160 \ {S}\ {cm}^{2} \ {mol}^{-1}{/tex} respectively. The value of limiting molar conductivity of {tex}{Al}_{2}\left({SO}_{4}\right)_{3}{/tex} will be:

a. {tex}198 \ {S} \ {cm}^{2} \ {mol}^{-1}{/tex}
b. {tex}858 \ {S} \ {cm}^{2} \ {mol}^{-1}{/tex}
c. {tex}588 \ {S} \ {cm}^{2} \ {mol}^{-1}{/tex}
d. {tex}891 \ {S} \ {cm}^{2} \ {mol}^{-1}{/tex}
1

SECTION D

The following questions are case-based questions. Read the case carefully and answer the questions that follow.
29.

In a galvanic cell, chemical energy of a redox reaction is converted into electrical energy, whereas in an electrolytic cell the redox reaction occurs on passing electricity. The simplest galvanic cell is in which Zn rod is placed in a solution of {tex}{ZnSO}_{4}{/tex} and Cu rod is placed in a solution of {tex}{CuSO}_{4}{/tex}. The two rods are connected by a metallic wire through a voltmeter. The two solutions are joined by a salt bridge. The difference between the two electrode potentials of the two electrodes is known as electromotive force. In the process of electrolysis, the decomposition of a substance takes place by passing an electric current. One mole of electric charge when passed through a cell will discharge half a mole of a divalent metal ion such as {tex}{Cu}^{2+}{/tex}. This was first formulated by Faraday in the form of laws of electrolysis.
Answer the following questions:

  1. What is the function of a salt bridge in a galvanic cell? (1)
  2. When does galvanic cell behave like an electrolytic cell? (1)
  3. Can copper sulphate solution be stored in a pot made of zinc? Explain with the help of the value of {tex}{E}^{\circ}{/tex} cell. (2)
    ({tex}{E}^{\circ} {Cu}^{2+} / {Cu}=0.34 {~V}{/tex})
    ({tex}{E}^{\circ} {Zn}^{2+} / {Zn}=-0.76 {~V}{/tex})

    OR

    How much charge in terms of Faraday is required for the following: (2)
    1. 1 mol of {tex}{MnO}_{4}^{-}{/tex}to {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex}
    2. 1 mol of {tex}{H}_{2} {O}{/tex} to {tex}{O}_{2}{/tex}
 4
30.

The nature of bonding, structure of the coordination compound can be explained to some extent by valence bond theory. The central metal atom/ion makes available a number of vacant orbitals equal to its coordination number. The appropriate atomic orbitals (s, p and d) of the metal hybridise to give a set of equivalent orbitals of definite geometry such as square planar, tetrahedral, octahedral and so on. A strong covalent bond is formed only when the orbitals overlap to the maximum extent. The d-orbitals involved in the hybridisation may be either inner d-orbitals i.e. ({tex}{n}-1{/tex}) d or outer d-orbitals i.e. nd. The complexes formed are called inner orbital complex (low spin complex) and outer orbital complex (high spin complex) respectively. Further, the complexes can be paramagnetic or diamagnetic in nature. The drawbacks of this theory are that this involves number of assumptions and also does not explain the colour of the complex.
Answer the following questions:

  1. Predict whether {tex}\left[{CoF}_{6}\right]^{3-}{/tex} is diamagnetic or paramagnetic and why? (1)
    [Atomic number : {tex}{Co}=27{/tex}]
  2. What is the coordination number of Co in {tex}\left[{Co}({en})_{2} {Cl}_{2}\right]^{+}{/tex}? (1)
    1. Write the IUPAC name of the given complex:
      {tex}\left[{Pt}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{2} {Cl}_{2}\right]^{2+}{/tex}
    2. Explain {tex}\left[{Co}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}{/tex} is an inner orbital or outer orbital complex. (2)

      OR

      Using valence bond theory, deduce the shape and hybridisation of {tex}\left[{Ni}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}{/tex} [Atomic number of {tex}{Ni}=28{/tex}] (2)
 4

{tex} {k}=\frac{2 \cdot 303}{{t}} \log \frac{[{R}]_0}{[{R}]} {/tex}
For 99.9 % completion
{tex} \text { Let }[R]_0=100 {/tex}
{tex} {[R]=100-99.9 \%=0.1} {/tex}
{tex} {t}_{99.9 \%}=\frac{2.303}{{k}} \log \frac{100}{0.1} {/tex}

{tex} {t}_{50 \%}=\frac{2 \cdot 303}{{k}} \log \frac{100}{50} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{2 \cdot 303}{{k}} \log 2 {/tex}

Divide (i) by (ii)
{tex} \frac{t_{99.9 \%}}{t_{50 \%}}=\frac{\frac{2.303}{k} \times 3}{\frac{2.303}{k} \times 0.3010} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{t_{99.9 \%}}{t_{50 \%}}=10 {/tex}

(ii)

What is Lanthanoid contraction?

Solution:

The overall decrease in atomic and ionic radii from lanthanum to lutetium (the lanthanoid contraction) is a unique feature in the chemistry of the lanthanoids. This steady decrease in atomic and ionic size with the increase in atomic number is called Lanthanoid contraction. This causes the radii of the members of the third transition series to be very similar to those of the corresponding members of the second series.

(iii)

Why first ionisation enthalpy of chromium is lower than that of Zn?

Solution:

The electronic configuration of chromium and zinc are respectively:
Cr(24) = [Ar] 3d54s2
Zn(30) = [Ar]3d104s2
It is easy to remove electron from 4 s1 -orbital (unpaired) rather than from 4s2 -orbital (paired). Therefore, first ionisation enthalpy of Cr is less than Zn.

Solution: The overall decrease in atomic and ionic radii from lanthanum to lutetium (the lanthanoid contraction) is a unique feature in the chemistry of the lanthanoids. This steady decrease in atomic and ionic size with the increase in atomic number is called Lanthanoid contraction. This causes the radii of the members of the third transition series to be very similar to those of the corresponding members of the second series. 1 (iii) Why first ionisation enthalpy of chromium is lower than that of Zn?
